name: Nekhbet
description: Nekhbet was an early predynastic local goddess in Egyptian mythology, who became the patron deity of the city of Nekheb (modern El Kab). Ultimately, she was adopted as the protector of Upper Egypt, and consequently one of the two patron deities for all of Ancient Egypt when it was unified. The other was Wadjet, the cobra goddess of Lower Egypt. Nekhbet was usually depicted as a white Egyptian vulture with her wings spread above the pharaoh, offering protection. She could also be represented as a woman with the head of a vulture, sometimes wearing the White Crown of Upper Egypt. As a protector goddess, she was believed to be a fierce defender of the pharaoh and the state, warding off enemies and offering divine guidance. Her role extended to childbirth, where she was seen as a protector of mothers and newborns. Nekhbet was associated with royalty, motherhood, and protection, embodying the power and stability of the Egyptian kingdom. Her worship was widespread throughout Egypt, and she was often depicted alongside other important deities such as Ra and Horus, highlighting her significance in the Egyptian pantheon.
subtitle: Vulture Goddess of Protection and Royalty

keyMyths.title: Nekhbet's Protection of the Pharaoh
keyMyths.description: Nekhbet is often depicted hovering above the pharaoh, spreading her wings to protect him from harm and guide him to victory. This symbolizes her role as the divine protector of the Egyptian ruler and the kingdom.

keyMyths.title: Unification of Egypt
keyMyths.description: With the unification of Upper and Lower Egypt, Nekhbet and Wadjet became the two patron deities representing the unified kingdom. Their presence symbolized the harmony and balance of the two lands.

keyMyths.title: Nekhbet and Childbirth
keyMyths.description: Nekhbet was invoked during childbirth to protect both the mother and the newborn child. Her presence ensured a safe delivery and the well-being of the new generation.
keyMyths.source: Ancient Egyptian medical texts and amulets

sources.author: Geraldine Pinch
